metadata.title: FDA Labeling (Adverse Reactions / PLR) and SUPAC-MR Bioequivalence Rules

sources.topic: Adverse Reactions Section of Labeling for Human Prescription Drug and Biological Products — Content and Format (January 2006, CDER/CBER)
SUPAC-MR: Modified Release Solid Oral Dosage Forms — Scale-Up and Postapproval Changes: CMC, In Vitro Dissolution Testing and In Vivo Bioequivalence Documentation (September 1997, CDER)

rules.rule_text: The ADVERSE REACTIONS section must include separate lists for adverse reactions identified from clinical trials and for those identified from spontaneous (postmarketing) reports.
rules.source_section: III. Adverse Reactions Section — Content and Format
rules.source_quote: Separate lists are required for adverse reactions identified from clinical trials (§ 201.57(c)(7)(ii)(A)) and those identified from spontaneous reports after a drug has been marketed (§ 201.57(c)(7)(ii)(B)).

rules.rule_text: The ADVERSE REACTIONS section must list the adverse reactions that occur with the drug and with drugs in the same pharmacologically active and chemically related class, if applicable.
rules.source_section: III. Adverse Reactions Section — Content and Format
rules.source_quote: The ADVERSE REACTIONS section is required to list the adverse reactions that occur with the drug and with drugs in the same pharmacologically active and chemically related class, if applicable (§ 201.57(c)(7)(i)).
rules.exceptions: Applicable only where pharmacologically related class exists

rules.rule_text: The beginning of the ADVERSE REACTIONS section must identify the most clinically significant adverse reactions and cross-reference all serious and otherwise important adverse reactions described in other labeling sections, especially BOXED WARNING or WARNINGS AND PRECAUTIONS.
rules.source_section: III.A. Making the Most Clinically Important Information Accessible
rules.source_quote: Identify and cross-reference all serious and otherwise important adverse reactions described in greater detail in other labeling sections, especially BOXED WARNING or WARNINGS AND PRECAUTIONS (e.g., see WARNINGS AND PRECAUTIONS (5.1)).

rules.rule_text: The beginning of the ADVERSE REACTIONS section must identify the most commonly occurring adverse reactions, defined as those occurring at a rate of 10 percent or greater in the treatment group and at a rate at least twice the placebo rate.
rules.source_section: III.A. Making the Most Clinically Important Information Accessible
rules.source_quote: Identify the most commonly occurring adverse reactions (e.g., all adverse reactions occurring at a rate of 10 percent or greater in the treatment group and at a rate at least twice the placebo rate).

rules.rule_text: The beginning of the ADVERSE REACTIONS section must identify adverse reactions that resulted in a significant rate of discontinuation or other clinical intervention in clinical trials.
rules.source_section: III.A. Making the Most Clinically Important Information Accessible
rules.source_quote: Identify adverse reactions, if any, that resulted in a significant rate of discontinuation or other clinical intervention (e.g., dosage adjustment, need for other therapy to treat an adverse reaction) in clinical trials.

rules.rule_text: The ADVERSE REACTIONS section must include a listing of all adverse reactions from clinical trials that occurred at or above a specified rate appropriate to the drug's safety database, plus a separate listing of those below the specified rate for which there is a causal relationship basis.
rules.source_section: III.B. Adverse Reactions From Clinical Trials
rules.source_quote: The ADVERSE REACTIONS section must include a listing of all such reactions that occurred at or above a specified rate that is appropriate to the drug's safety database (see III.B.3), a separate listing of those adverse reactions that occurred below the specified rate, but for which there is some basis to believe there is a causal relationship between the drug and the event (see III.B.4).

rules.rule_text: Presentation of adverse reactions from clinical trials must be preceded by a description of the clinical trial database, including overall exposure (number of patients, dose, schedule, duration), demographics of the exposed population, trial designs, and critical exclusions from the safety database.
rules.source_quote: The presentation of adverse reactions information identified from clinical trials must be preceded by information necessary to interpret the adverse reactions (§ 201.57(c)(7)(i)). This information would ordinarily include a description of the overall clinical trial database from which adverse reaction data have been drawn, including a discussion of overall exposure (number of patients, dose, schedule, duration), demographics of the exposed population, designs of the trials in which exposure occurred (e.g., placebo-controlled, active-controlled), and any critical exclusions from the safety database.

rules.rule_text: The adverse reactions table for common reactions must include the rate of occurrence of an adverse reaction for the drug and any comparators (active- or placebo-controls), unless such data cannot be determined or presenting comparator rates would be misleading.
rules.source_section: III.B.3. Presentation of Common Adverse Reactions
rules.source_quote: The listing must include the rate of occurrence of an adverse reaction for the drug and any comparators (active- or placebo-controls), unless such data cannot be determined or presenting the rates for a comparator would be misleading (§ 201.57(c)(7)(ii)(A)).
rules.exceptions: Data cannot be determined, or presenting comparator rates would be misleading

rules.rule_text: The primary data source for the common adverse reactions table must be placebo-controlled and/or dose-response studies if available and sufficiently large; if unavailable, use active-controlled data; if concurrently controlled data are unavailable, overall rates from well-monitored single-arm databases may be used.
rules.source_quote: The data in the listing of common adverse reactions should be derived from placebo-controlled and/or dose-response studies if these data are available and the databases are sufficiently large to be informative. If these data are unavailable or not sufficiently informative, the primary table should be based on active-controlled data. If concurrently controlled data are unavailable, overall rates from well-monitored, single-arm databases can be used to provide some indication of what was observed in treated patients.

rules.rule_text: For serious adverse events that are unusual in the absence of drug therapy (e.g., liver failure, agranulocytosis, rhabdomyolysis), these events must generally be listed in the adverse reactions section even if there are only one or two reported events, unless a causal relationship can be clearly excluded.
rules.source_quote: For serious adverse events that are unusual in the absence of drug therapy (e.g., liver failure, agranulocytosis, rhabdomyolysis, idiopathic thrombocytopenic purpura, intussusception), there is a basis to believe there is a causal relationship between the event and the drug at a very low rate of occurrence. Therefore, these events are generally listed in the adverse reactions section even if there are only one or two reported events, unless it is clear that a causal relationship can be excluded.
rules.exceptions: A causal relationship can be clearly excluded

rules.rule_text: Adverse reaction rates must ordinarily be derived from all reported adverse events of that type in the database used. Determining a rate based on a subset of reported events believed by individual investigators to be causally related to drug exposure is discouraged.
rules.source_quote: The rate of an identified adverse reaction is ordinarily derived from all reported adverse events of that type in the database used. Determining a rate based on a subset of reported events that individual investigators believe to be causally related to drug exposure is discouraged. Excluding events from the rate calculation based on the judgment of individual investigators introduces bias and inconsistency in rate determinations.

rules.rule_text: Nonspecific terms that lack a commonly understood or precise meaning, such as 'well-tolerated,' 'rare,' 'infrequent,' and 'frequent,' must not be used in characterizing overall adverse reaction experience. Specific frequency ranges (e.g., adverse reactions occurring in < 1/500) must be used instead.
rules.source_quote: In characterizing overall adverse reaction experience, nonspecific terms that lack a commonly understood or precise meaning are discouraged, as use of such terms can be misleading. For example, the phrase well-tolerated is a vague and subjective judgment about a drug's adverse reaction profile for which there are no commonly understood parameters. In addition, the terms rare, infrequent, and frequent do not provide meaningful information about the frequency of occurrence of adverse reactions. Specific frequency ranges (e.g., adverse reactions occurring in < 1/500) provide more precise information about incidence.

rules.rule_text: Comparative safety claims for drugs in terms of frequency, severity, or character of adverse reaction must be based on data from adequate and well-controlled studies (as defined in 21 CFR 314.126), unless this requirement is waived.
rules.source_quote: Comparative safety claims for drugs in terms of frequency, severity, or character of adverse reaction must be based on data from adequate and well-controlled studies (as defined in 21 CFR 314.126), unless this requirement is waived (§ 201.57(c)(7)(iii)).
rules.exceptions: Requirement can be waived under 21 CFR 201.58 or 21 CFR 314.126(c)

rules.rule_text: Within a listing, adverse reactions must be categorized by body system, by severity of reaction, in order of decreasing frequency, or by a combination of these. Within each category, adverse reactions must be listed in decreasing order of frequency. If frequency cannot be reliably determined, adverse reactions must be listed in decreasing order of severity.
rules.source_quote: Within a listing, adverse reactions must be categorized by body system, by severity of reaction, in order of decreasing frequency, or by a combination of these, as appropriate. Within a category, adverse reactions must be listed in decreasing order of frequency. If frequency cannot be reliably determined, adverse reactions must be listed in decreasing order of severity (§ 201.57(c)(7)(ii)).

rules.rule_text: The frequency cutoff for the listing of common adverse reactions table must be appropriate to the safety database and must be noted in the listing or table header, in the accompanying text, or in a footnote.
rules.source_quote: The frequency cutoff for the listing of common adverse reactions identified from clinical trials (usually the adverse reactions table) must be appropriate to the safety database (§ 201.57(c)(7)(ii)(A)). Factors that could influence selection of a frequency cut-off include the size of the safety database, the designs of the trials in the database, and the nature of the indication. The frequency cutoff should be noted in the listing or table header, in the text accompanying the listing or table, or in a footnote.

rules.rule_text: The denominator (N = number of patients) must be provided for each column in a table or listing of adverse reactions from clinical trials, except for the listing of adverse reactions identified from postmarketing spontaneous reports.
rules.source_quote: The denominator (N = number of patients) should be provided for each column in a table or listing, except for the listing of adverse reactions identified from postmarketing spontaneous reports (see III.C).
rules.exceptions: Listing of adverse reactions from postmarketing spontaneous reports is exempt

rules.rule_text: Adverse reaction rates expressed in percentages must ordinarily be rounded to the nearest integer, except for particularly serious adverse reactions occurring at low rates in a large study where fractions of a percent may be meaningful.
rules.source_quote: Adverse reaction rates expressed in percentages should ordinarily be rounded to the nearest integer. An exception would be for particularly serious adverse reactions (e.g., stroke, intracranial hemorrhage, agranulocytosis) occurring at low rates in a large study where fractions of a percent may be meaningful.
rules.exceptions: Particularly serious adverse reactions occurring at low rates in large studies

rules.rule_text: Adverse reactions for which the placebo rate equals or exceeds the drug rate (after rounding) must not be included in the ADVERSE REACTIONS section unless there is a compelling factor (e.g., timing) suggesting the drug caused the event.
rules.source_section: V.I. Adverse Reaction Rates for Drug Less Than for Placebo
rules.source_quote: Adverse reactions for which the placebo rate equals or exceeds the rate for the drug (after rounding) should not be included in the ADVERSE REACTIONS section unless there is some compelling factor (e.g., timing) that suggests that the event is caused by the drug. In that case, the adverse reaction should be discussed in the commentary following the table.
rules.exceptions: Compelling factor (e.g., timing) suggesting drug causation

rules.rule_text: The ADVERSE REACTIONS section must separately list adverse reactions identified from domestic and foreign spontaneous reports. This listing must be separate from the listing of adverse reactions identified from clinical trials and must be preceded by a standardized interpretive statement.
rules.source_section: III.C. Presentation of Adverse Reaction Information From Spontaneous Reports
rules.source_quote: The ADVERSE REACTIONS section must list adverse reactions identified from domestic and foreign spontaneous reports (§ 201.57(c)(7)(ii)(B)). This listing must be separate from the listing of adverse reactions identified in clinical trials (§ 201.57(c)(7)(ii)(B)) and must also be preceded by information necessary to interpret the adverse reactions (§ 201.57(c)(7)(i)).

rules.rule_text: When an adverse reaction identified from spontaneous reporting is included in labeling, the number of spontaneous reports ordinarily must not be cited, as the number can quickly become outdated. If the number of reports is cited, the period of observation must be stated.
rules.source_section: III.C. Presentation of Adverse Reaction Information From Spontaneous Reports
rules.source_quote: When an adverse reaction identified from spontaneous reporting is included in the labeling, the number of spontaneous reports ordinarily is not cited, because the number can quickly become outdated. If the number of reports is cited, the period of observation should be stated.
rules.exceptions: If number cited, observation period must be stated

rules.rule_text: For adverse reactions with significant clinical implications (most commonly occurring, causing discontinuation, or requiring monitoring), the listings must be supplemented with additional details about the nature, frequency, severity, duration, dose-response, and demographic characteristics.
rules.source_section: III.B.5. Commentary on Listings of Common and Less Common Adverse Reactions
rules.source_quote: For adverse reactions with significant clinical implications (e.g., those that are most commonly occurring, that result in discontinuation or dose modification, or that require monitoring), the listings of common and less common adverse reactions must be supplemented with additional details about the nature, frequency, severity, dose-response, and demographic characteristics of the adverse reaction, to the extent data are available and important (§ 201.57(c)(7)(ii)(A)).
rules.exceptions: Only to the extent data are available and important

rules.rule_text: The commentary on adverse reactions must identify clinically significant adverse reactions that exhibit a dose response relationship.
rules.source_quote: The commentary must identify clinically significant adverse reactions that exhibit a dose response (§ 201.57(c)(7)(ii)(A)).

rules.rule_text: The adverse reactions commentary must include clinically important information about observed differences, or lack of observed differences, in adverse reactions in various demographic groups (e.g., age, racial, sex). Where reliable information is unavailable, the fact and the reason (e.g., trials not designed or powered to detect such differences) must be disclosed.
rules.source_quote: The commentary must include clinically important information about observed differences, or lack of observed differences, in adverse reactions in various demographic groups (e.g., age, racial, sex) (§ 201.57(c)(7)(ii)(A)). Where there is no reliable information on differences or similarities in adverse reaction profiles among demographic subgroups, that fact should be disclosed, along with an explanation of why such information is unavailable.

rules.rule_text: Applicants must update the labeling when new information becomes available that causes the labeling to become inaccurate, false, or misleading. Applicants are urged to review the ADVERSE REACTIONS section at least annually.
rules.source_quote: Applicants are urged to review at least annually the content of the ADVERSE REACTIONS section to ensure that the information remains current... the applicant must update the labeling when new information becomes available that causes the labeling to become inaccurate, false, or misleading (21 CFR 201.56(a)(2)).

rules.rule_text: Adverse reactions must be classified using meaningful and specific terms, with a common classification scheme across all studies in the safety database. Related terms representing the same phenomenon (e.g., sedation, somnolence, drowsiness) must ordinarily be grouped together as a single adverse reaction.
rules.source_quote: Adverse reactions should be classified using meaningful and specific terms that best communicate the nature and significance of the reaction. There should ordinarily be a common classification scheme across all studies in the safety database. Events that are reported under different terms in the database, but that represent the same phenomenon (e.g., sedation, somnolence, drowsiness) should ordinarily be grouped together as a single adverse reaction to avoid diluting or obscuring the true effect.

rules.rule_text: For postapproval changes to modified release solid oral dosage forms, sponsors must classify changes into one of three levels (Level 1, 2, or 3) based on their potential impact on formulation quality and performance, and apply the corresponding CMC, dissolution, and bioequivalence documentation requirements.
rules.source_quote: The guidance defines (1) levels of change, (2) recommended chemistry, manufacturing, and controls (CMC) tests for each level of change, (3) recommended in vitro dissolution tests and/or in vivo bioequivalence tests for each level of change; and (4) documentation that should support the change.

rules.rule_text: For SUPAC-MR changes, sponsors must commit to conduct long-term stability studies through the expiration dating period, according to the approved protocol, on the first or first three production batches (depending on the level of change) and report the results in annual reports.
rules.source_quote: A commitment should be included to conduct long-term stability studies through the expiration dating period, according to the approved protocol, on the first or first three (see text for details) production batches and to report the results in the annual reports.

rules.rule_text: For Level 1 changes to nonrelease controlling excipients, the total additive effect of all nonrelease controlling excipient changes must not be more than 5% (w/w) of the total target dosage form weight. Individual excipient limits apply (Filler ±5%, Disintegrant Starch ±3%, Disintegrant Other ±1%, Binder ±0.5%, Lubricant Ca or Mg Stearate ±0.25%, Lubricant Other ±1%, Glidant Talc ±1%, Glidant Other ±0.1%, Film Coat ±1%).
rules.source_section: III.A. Level 1 Change — Nonrelease Controlling Excipient
rules.source_quote: Changes in nonrelease controlling excipients, expressed as percentage (w/w) of total formulation, less than or equal to the following percent ranges: Filler ±5, Disintegrant Starch ±3, Disintegrant Other ±1, Binder ±0.5, Lubricant Ca or Mg Stearate ±0.25, Lubricant Other ±1, Glidant Talc ±1, Glidant Other ±0.1, Film Coat ±1... The total additive effect of all nonrelease controlling excipient changes should not be more than 5%.
rules.exceptions: Changes involving only different color, flavor, or preservative may be subject to waiver per 21 CFR 320.22(d)(4)

rules.rule_text: Level 1 nonrelease controlling excipient changes require Annual Report filing only (all information including long-term stability data). No dissolution or bioequivalence documentation beyond application/compendial requirements is needed.
rules.source_quote: Annual report (all information including long-term stability data).

rules.rule_text: For Level 2 changes to nonrelease controlling excipients, the total additive effect of all nonrelease controlling excipient changes must not change by more than 10% (w/w). Individual excipient limits (two-fold increase over Level 1) apply: Filler ±10%, Disintegrant Starch ±6%, Disintegrant Other ±2%, Binder ±1%, Lubricant Ca or Mg Stearate ±0.5%, Lubricant Other ±2%, Glidant Talc ±2%, Glidant Other ±0.2%, Film Coat ±2%.
rules.source_section: III.B. Level 2 Change — Nonrelease Controlling Excipient
rules.source_quote: Changes in nonrelease controlling excipients, expressed as percentage (w/w) of total formulation, greater than those listed above for a level 1 change, but less than or equal to the following percent ranges (which represent a two-fold increase over level 1 changes): Filler ±10, Disintegrant Starch ±6, Disintegrant Other ±2, Binder ±1, Lubricant Ca or Mg Stearate ±0.5, Lubricant Other ±2, Glidant Talc ±2, Glidant Other ±0.2, Film Coat ±2... The total additive effect of all nonrelease controlling excipient changes should not change by more than 10%.

rules.rule_text: Level 2 nonrelease controlling excipient changes require a Prior Approval Supplement (all information including accelerated stability data) and Annual Report (long-term stability data). Dissolution documentation requires multipoint dissolution profiles in at least 3 additional media for extended release and the standard acid/buffer stages for delayed release.
rules.source_section: III.B. Level 2 Change — Filing and Test Documentation
rules.source_quote: Extended release: In addition to application/compendial release requirements, multipoint dissolution profiles should be obtained in three other media, for example, in water, 0.1N HCl, and USP buffer media at pH 4.5, and 6.8 for the changed drug product and the biobatch or marketed batch (unchanged drug product)... Prior approval supplement (all information including accelerated stability data); annual report (long-term stability data).
rules.exceptions: In vitro/in vivo correlation established: only application/compendial dissolution testing required

rules.rule_text: Level 3 nonrelease controlling excipient changes (exceeding Level 2 ranges, including addition or deletion of an excipient) require a Prior Approval Supplement with accelerated stability data. A single-dose bioequivalence study is required and may be waived only in the presence of an established in vitro/in vivo correlation.
rules.source_section: III.C. Level 3 Change — Nonrelease Controlling Excipient
rules.source_quote: Level 3 changes are those that are likely to have a significant impact on formulation quality and performance... A single-dose bioequivalence study (3). The bioequivalence study may be waived in the presence of an established in vitro/in vivo correlation (6).
rules.exceptions: Established in vitro/in vivo correlation permits waiver of in vivo BE study

rules.rule_text: For Level 1 changes to release controlling excipients, the total additive effect of all release controlling excipient changes must not be more than 5% w/w of the total release controlling excipient content in the original approved formulation. Changes must be expressed as percentage (w/w) of total release controlling excipient(s) less than or equal to 5% w/w.
rules.source_section: IV.A. Level 1 Change — Release Controlling Excipient
rules.source_quote: Changes in the release controlling excipient(s), expressed as percentage (w/w) of total release controlling excipient(s) in the formulation less than or equal to 5% w/w of total release controlling excipient content in the modified release solid oral dosage form... The total additive effect of all release controlling excipient changes should not be more than 5% w/w of the total release controlling excipients in the original approved formulation.

rules.rule_text: For Level 2 changes to release controlling excipients (greater than 5% but not more than 10% w/w of total release controlling excipient content), narrow therapeutic range drugs require a single-dose bioequivalence study; non-narrow therapeutic range drugs require no bioequivalence documentation. Narrow therapeutic range drugs require stability data from three batches; non-narrow require one batch.
rules.source_section: IV.B. Level 2 Change — Release Controlling Excipient
rules.source_quote: Changes in the release controlling excipient(s), expressed as percentage (w/w) of total release controlling excipient(s) in the formulation, greater than those listed above for a level 1 change, but less than or equal to 10% w/w of total release controlling excipient content in the modified release solid oral dosage form... Narrow therapeutic range drugs: A single-dose bioequivalence study (3). The bioequivalence study may be waived in the presence of an established in vitro/in vivo correlation (6).
rules.exceptions: Established IVIVC permits waiver of in vivo BE for narrow therapeutic range drugs

rules.rule_text: Level 3 changes to release controlling excipients (addition/deletion of release controlling excipients, or changes greater than 10% w/w of total release controlling excipient content) require a Prior Approval Supplement with stability data from three batches and a single-dose bioequivalence study. The BE study may be waived only with established IVIVC.
rules.source_section: IV.C. Level 3 Change — Release Controlling Excipient
rules.source_quote: Level 3 changes are those that are likely to have a significant impact on formulation quality and performance affecting all therapeutic ranges of the drug... Addition or deletion of release controlling excipients(s)... Changes in the release controlling excipient(s)... greater than those listed above for a level 2 change (i.e., greater than 10% w/w of total release controlling excipient content)... A single-dose bioequivalence study (3). The bioequivalence study may be waived in the presence of an established in vitro/in vivo correlation (6).
rules.exceptions: Established in vitro/in vivo correlation permits waiver of in vivo BE study

rules.rule_text: New manufacturing locations must have had a satisfactory current good manufacturing practice (cGMP) inspection before a site change is implemented.
rules.source_quote: New manufacturing locations should have had a satisfactory current good manufacturing practice (cGMP) inspection.

rules.rule_text: Level 2 site changes (contiguous campus or adjacent city blocks, same equipment/SOPs/personnel) must be submitted as a Changes Being Effected (CBE) supplement with one batch of three months' accelerated stability data plus long-term stability data of first production batch reported in the annual report. Multipoint dissolution profiles are required for extended and delayed release forms.
rules.source_quote: Level 2 changes consist of site changes within a contiguous campus, or between facilities in adjacent city blocks, where the same equipment, SOPs, environmental conditions (e.g., temperature and humidity) and controls, and personnel common to both manufacturing sites are used... Changes Being Effected supplement (all information including accelerated stability data); annual report (long-term stability data).
rules.exceptions: In vitro/in vivo correlation established: only application/compendial dissolution required

rules.rule_text: Level 3 site changes (change to a different campus not on the same contiguous site and not in adjacent city blocks) require a Prior Approval Supplement and a single-dose bioequivalence study. The BE study may be waived in the presence of an established in vitro/in vivo correlation.
rules.source_quote: Level 3 changes consist of a change in manufacturing site to a different campus... Prior approval supplement (all information including accelerated stability test data); annual report (long-term stability data)... A single-dose bioequivalence study (3). The bioequivalence study may be waived in the presence of an established in vitro/in vivo correlation (6).
rules.exceptions: Established IVIVC permits waiver of in vivo BE study

rules.rule_text: Scale-down below 100,000 dosage units is not covered by SUPAC-MR guidance. Level 1 batch size change is defined as up to and including a factor of ten times the size of the pilot/biobatch. Level 2 batch size change is defined as beyond a factor of ten times the size of the pilot/biobatch.
rules.source_quote: Scale-down below 100,000 dosage units is not covered by this guidance... Change in batch size, up to and including a factor of ten times the size of the pilot/biobatch [Level 1]... Changes in batch size beyond a factor of ten times the size of the pilot/biobatch [Level 2].

rules.rule_text: Level 2 batch size changes (beyond factor of ten times pilot/biobatch) require a Changes Being Effected (CBE) supplement with one batch of three months' accelerated stability data. Multipoint dissolution profiles are required for extended and delayed release forms. No in vivo bioequivalence documentation is required.
rules.source_quote: Changes in batch size beyond a factor of ten times the size of the pilot/biobatch where (1) the equipment used to produce the test batch(es) is of the same design and operating principles; (2) the batch(es) is manufactured in full compliance with cGMPs; and (3) the same SOPs and controls as well as the same formulation and manufacturing procedures are used... Changes Being Effected supplement (all information including accelerated stability data); annual report (long-term stability data).
rules.exceptions: Established IVIVC: only application/compendial dissolution required

rules.rule_text: Level 2 manufacturing equipment changes (change to equipment of a different design and different operating principles) require a Prior Approval Supplement with accelerated stability data. Multipoint dissolution profiles are required for both extended and delayed release forms. No in vivo bioequivalence documentation is required.
rules.source_quote: Change in equipment to a different design and different operating principles... Prior approval supplement with justification for change (all information including accelerated stability data); annual report (long-term stability data).
rules.exceptions: Established IVIVC: only application/compendial dissolution required

rules.rule_text: Level 3 manufacturing process changes (change in the type of process, such as from wet granulation to direct compression of dry powder) require a Prior Approval Supplement with stability data from three batches and a single-dose bioequivalence study. The BE study may be waived only with established IVIVC.
rules.source_quote: This category includes change in the type of process used in the manufacture of the product, such as a change from wet granulation to direct compression of dry powder... Three batches with three months' accelerated stability data reported in prior approval supplement and long-term stability data of first three production batches reported in annual report... A single-dose bioequivalence study (3). The bioequivalence study may be waived in the presence of an established in vitro/in vivo correlation (6).
rules.exceptions: Established IVIVC permits waiver of in vivo BE study

rules.rule_text: For extended release forms, multipoint dissolution profiles for SUPAC-MR changes (Levels 2 and 3) must be obtained in three additional media (water, 0.1N HCl, and USP buffer at pH 4.5 and 6.8) in addition to application/compendial requirements. Sampling must be performed at 1, 2, and 4 hours and every two hours thereafter until 80% of the drug is released or an asymptote is reached.
rules.source_quote: Extended release: In addition to application/compendial release requirements, multipoint dissolution profiles should be obtained in three other media, for example, in water, 0.1N HCl, and USP buffer media at pH 4.5, and 6.8 for the changed drug product and the biobatch or marketed batch (unchanged drug product). Adequate sampling should be performed, for example, at 1, 2, and 4 hours and every two hours thereafter until either 80% of the drug from the drug product is released or an asymptote is reached.
rules.exceptions: Established IVIVC: only application/compendial dissolution required

rules.rule_text: For delayed release forms, dissolution tests for SUPAC-MR changes must be performed in 0.1N HCl for 2 hours (acid stage) followed by testing in USP buffer media in the range of pH 4.5-7.5 (buffer stage) under standard conditions and two additional agitation speeds. For rotating basket (Apparatus 1): 50, 100, 150 rpm; for rotating paddle (Apparatus 2): 50, 75, 100 rpm.
rules.source_quote: Delayed release: In addition to application/compendial release requirements, dissolution tests should be performed in 0.1 N HCl for 2 hours (acid stage) followed by testing in USP buffer media, in the range of pH 4.5-7.5 (buffer stage) under standard (application/compendial) test conditions and two additional agitation speeds using the application/compendial test apparatus (three additional test conditions). If the application/compendial test apparatus is the rotating basket method (Apparatus 1), a rotation speed of 50, 100, and 150 rpm may be used, and if the application/compendial test apparatus is the rotating paddle method (Apparatus 2), a rotation speed of 50, 75, and 100 rpm may be used.
rules.exceptions: Established IVIVC: only application/compendial dissolution required

rules.rule_text: When dissolution profile comparison is required, the f2 similarity factor must be calculated. An f2 value between 50 and 100 indicates similarity. The average difference at any dissolution sampling time point must not be greater than 15% between the changed and unchanged drug products. The reference profile must be derived from at least three consecutive recent batches of the unchanged drug product.
rules.source_section: Glossary — In Vitro Dissolution Profile Comparison
rules.source_quote: An f2 value between 50 and 100 suggests the two dissolution profiles are similar. Also, the average difference at any dissolution sampling time point should not be greater than 15% between the changed drug product and the biobatch or marketed batch (unchanged drug product) dissolution profiles. An appropriate reference for this comparison should represent an average dissolution profile derived from at least three consecutive recent batches of the unchanged drug product (biobatch or marketed batch).
rules.exceptions: f2 < 50: sponsor may submit justification in a Prior Approval Supplement with additional data and 90% CI analysis

rules.rule_text: For extended release dissolution testing, 12 individual dosage units must be tested for the changed drug product and the biobatch or marketed batch. Dissolution temperature must be 37 ± 0.5 degrees C. Dissolution volume must be 500-1000 mL. Sampling must be at 1, 2, and 4 hours and every two hours thereafter until 80% is released or an asymptote is reached.
rules.source_quote: Dissolution Testing: Extended release: Dissolution testing should be conducted on 12 individual dosage units for the changed drug product and the biobatch or marketed batch... Temperature: 37±0.5ºC... Units To Be Tested: 12... Dissolution Volume: 500-1000 mL... Sampling Schedule: Adequate sampling should be performed, for example at 1, 2, and 4 hours, and every two hours thereafter until either 80% of the drug is released or an asymptote is reached.

rules.rule_text: The biobatch for modified release solid oral dosage forms must be 10% or greater than the proposed commercial production batch size, or at least 100,000 units, whichever is greater. The bioequivalence study must use the reference listed drug (RLD) product and/or innovator drug product as reference.
rules.source_quote: Biobatch: The lot of drug product formulated for purposes of pharmacokinetic evaluation in a bioavailability/bioequivalency study. For modified release solid oral, this batch should be 10% or greater than the proposed commercial production batch or at least 100,000 units, whichever is greater. Bioequivalence Studies For Modified Release Drug Product: Refer to the OGD Guidance (3). The bioequivalence study should be conducted using the reference listed drug (RLD) product and/or the innovator drug product as the reference and the test product should be the product (generic or innovator) which has undergone postapproval change.

rules.rule_text: An established in vitro/in vivo correlation (IVIVC) can be used to waive in vivo bioequivalence testing at any level of postapproval change described in SUPAC-MR, provided that changes in release controlling excipients in the formulation are within the range of release controlling excipients of the established correlation.
rules.source_quote: For modified release dosage forms, changes in release controlling excipients in the formulation should be within the range of release controlling excipients of the established correlation. In the presence of an established in vitro/in vivo correlation (6), only application/compendial dissolution testing need be performed. Also, an established in vitro/in vivo correlation can be used for any level of changes described in this guidance.
rules.exceptions: Changes in release controlling excipients must be within the range of the established IVIVC

rules.rule_text: For SUPAC-MR Level 2 changes requiring a Prior Approval Supplement (e.g., nonrelease excipient Level 2, process Level 2 at CBE), stability data must include one batch with three months' accelerated stability data. For Level 3 changes, three batches with three months' accelerated stability data are required.
rules.source_quote: One batch with three months accelerated stability data reported in prior approval supplement and long-term stability data of first production batch reported in annual report... [Level 3:] Significant body of information not available: Three batches with three months' accelerated stability data reported in prior approval supplement and long-term stability data of first three production batches reported in annual report.
rules.exceptions: Significant body of information available for Level 3: reduced to one batch for prior approval supplement

rules.rule_text: Pilot scale for modified release solid oral dosage forms is defined as at a minimum, one tenth of full production, or 100,000 tablets or capsules, whichever is larger.
rules.source_quote: Pilot Scale: The manufacture of either drug substance or drug product by a procedure fully representative of and simulating that used for full manufacturing scale. For solid oral dosage forms this is generally taken to be, at a minimum, one tenth that of full production, or 100,000 tablets or capsules, whichever is larger.

rules.rule_text: A 'significant body of information' for modified release solid oral dosage forms exists after five years of commercial experience for the original modified release solid oral drug product, or three years of commercial experience for any subsequent modified release solid oral drug product utilizing a similar drug release mechanism.
rules.source_quote: Modified Release Solid Oral Dosage Forms: A significant body of information should include, for 'Modified Release Solid Oral Dosage Forms,' a product-specific body of information. This product-specific body of information is likely to exist after five years of commercial experience for the original modified release solid oral drug product, or three years of commercial experience for any subsequent modified release solid oral drug product utilizing similar drug release mechanism.

rules.rule_text: Allowable excipient changes under SUPAC-MR must always be calculated based on the original approved target composition, not on the composition resulting from previous Level 1 or Level 2 changes. For products approved with only a range for excipients, the target value is the midpoint of the original approved application range.
rules.source_quote: Allowable changes in the composition should be based on the original approved target composition and not on previous level 1 changes in the composition. For products approved with only a range for excipients, the target value may be assumed to be the midpoint of the original approved application range.

rules.rule_text: When a manufacturer wishes to use manufacturing equipment not identical to the original manufacturing equipment in the approved application, appropriate validation studies must be conducted to demonstrate that the new equipment is similar to the original equipment.
rules.source_quote: If a manufacturer wishes to use manufacturing equipment that is not identical in every respect to the original manufacturing equipment used in the approved application, appropriate validation studies should be conducted to demonstrate that the new equipment is similar to the original equipment.

rules.rule_text: For comparison of multipoint dissolution profiles obtained in multiple media, similarity testing using the f2 factor must be performed pairwise (for the unchanged and changed product) in each individual medium. Only one point past the plateau of the profiles should be used in calculating the f2 value.
rules.source_section: Glossary — In Vitro Dissolution Profile Comparison
rules.source_quote: For comparison of multipoint dissolution profiles obtained in multiple media, similarity testing should be performed using pairwise dissolution profiles (i.e., for the unchanged and changed product) obtained in each individual medium. It is recommended that only one point past the plateau of the profiles be used in calculating the f2 value. A correction for a lag time prior to similarity testing should not be performed unless justified.
rules.exceptions: A lag time correction must not be performed unless justified

rules.rule_text: A stand-alone packaging operations site change using container/closure systems in the approved application may be submitted as a Changes Being Effected supplement, provided the facility has a current and satisfactory cGMP compliance profile with FDA for the type of packaging operation. If not, a Prior Approval Supplement is required.
rules.source_quote: A stand-alone packaging operations site change, using container(s)/closure(s) in the approved application, may be submitted as a Changes Being Effected supplement. The facility should also have a current and satisfactory cGMP compliance profile with the FDA for the type of packaging operation in question before submitting the supplement. If the facility has not received a satisfactory cGMP inspection for the type of packaging operation in question, a prior approval supplement is recommended.
rules.exceptions: Facility has current satisfactory cGMP compliance profile with FDA
